entry.title: Meet Me at SAPPHIRE — Let's Talk SAP Business Data Cloud 🚀
entry.content:
SAP SAPPHIRE is just around the corner, and I'm excited to share that I'll be presenting in Orlando this May! If you're heading to the event — or just curious about what's coming — here's a quick preview of what to expect.

My session: Introduction to SAP Business Data Cloud

SAP Business Data Cloud turned one year old — and what a year it's been. At SAPPHIRE 2026 in Orlando, I have the pleasure of hosting the introductory session on SAP Business Data Cloud. Think of it as your 20-minute guided tour of the product: what it is, how it's positioned, and where it fits into SAP's overall data and analytics strategy.

I'm running this session twice:

  • 📅 Monday, May 11 🕜 1:30 PM - 1:50 PM EDT
  • 📅 Tuesday, May 12 🕓 4:00 PM - 4:20 PM EDT

🔗  Link to the session: https://www.sap.com/events/sapphire/orlando/flow/sap/so26/catalog/page/catalog/session/1772570877449001qvJ7

The session is perfect for anyone who hasn't had a close look at SAP BDC yet. You'll get a clear overview of the full offering — from SAP Datasphere for data modeling and preparation, to SAP Analytics Cloud for analytics, planning, and consumption, all the way to Joule as the AI-powered interface layer. I'll also point you toward the deeper sessions covering topics like AI, analytics, and partner integrations — because with 30+ sessions on Business Data Cloud at this year's SAPPHIRE, there's plenty more to explore.

Come find me in Orlando

Beyond the sessions, I'll also be at the SAP Business Data Cloud booth — we have six demo stations and a great team ready to answer your questions. There are also hands-on sessions if you want to get your fingers on the keyboard. So, no excuse when you are in Orlando. Let's meet and have a chat.

Can't make it to Orlando? No problem:

➡️  I publish a monthly newsletter 📰 on the SAP Community covering all things BDC and Datasphere — check it out and follow me https://profile.sap.com/u/kpsauer or also on LinkedIn https://www.linkedin.com/in/klaus-peter-sauer/

➡️  Great content on YouTube 🎥, including the "New Era of Business Data" series covering all areas of SAP BDC including licensing
🔗  https://events.sap.com/data-analytics/en_us/home.html and my monthly top features video playlist
🔗  https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL3ZRUb1AKkpS8bwmAREXo78O2xccXzoiy

➡️  There's a free trial 🆓 of SAP Datasphere available — no excuses not to explore it yourself
🔗  https://www.sap.com/products/data-cloud/trial.html

One question for you 🫵

SAP Business Data Cloud is now roughly one year old. I'm curious: what capability or feature do you feel is still missing — and what value would it bring you? Drop your thoughts in the comments 👇.
I'd love to hear what the community is thinking, and it'll help shape where we focus next.

See you in Orlando! 🌴

entry.title: The Challenges You Face and How SAP Business Data Cloud Helps
entry.content:
Data stewards are accountable for data quality, compliance, and trust, but operate across fragmented systems, manual processes, and growing regulatory pressure—often without clear ownership or visibility.  For data stewards, SAP Business Data Cloud is not “more governance tooling.” It is a way to operationalize governance, scale trust, and turn stewardship into a measurable business enabler.

This is where SAP Business Data Cloud changes the conversation. It gives data stewards a governed, business‑ready foundation where business context, semantics, data, and usage all come together. Governance isn’t separate from analytics—it’s embedded in how data is shared and consumed.

Let’s talk a bit about problems and solutions:\

Fragmented data and inconsistent definitions

Your challenge:
Key business data is spread across SAP and non‑SAP systems, often defined differently by different teams. This makes it hard to enforce standards or build confidence in reporting and analytics.

How SAP Business Data Cloud helps:

  • Brings business data together with a consistent semantic layer
  • Helps align technical data with shared business definitions
  • Makes governed, trusted data easier to discover and use across analytics and AI scenarios

What this means for you:
Less time reconciling definitions. More confidence that people are working from the same understanding of the data.

Manual governance and limited visibility

Your challenge:
Governance work often lives in documents, spreadsheets, or disconnected tools, making it hard to track ownership, issues, and progress.

How SAP Business Data Cloud helps:

  • Connects governance concepts directly to live data assets and processes
  • Improves visibility into where data comes from, how it’s used, and who is responsible
  • Supports more consistent, repeatable governance practices instead of one‑off fixes

What this means for you:
You can focus on managing and improving data, not chasing information or enforcing rules manually.

Compliance pressure without operational support

Your challenge:
You’re expected to support regulatory and internal compliance, but controls are often indirect or hard to demonstrate during audits.

How SAP Business Data Cloud helps:

  • Enables clearer lineage, transparency, and traceability across data flows
  • Helps connect governance policies to actual data usage
  • Makes it easier to show how data is managed, protected, and governed in practice

What this means for you:
Stronger audit readiness and reduced risk—without slowing down the business.

Building trust with business and IT stakeholders

Your challenge:
Business users want speed. IT teams want stability. You need both to trust the data and the governance behind it.

How SAP Business Data Cloud helps:

  • Provides a shared foundation where business and technical teams work from the same governed data
  • Supports analytics and AI use cases without bypassing governance
  • Helps you communicate the value of governance in terms business leaders understand

What this means for you:
You gain credibility as a trusted partner, not a blocker—strengthening your influence in the buying and decision‑making process.

Again, it’s not about “more governance.” It’s about giving data stewards the structure, transparency, and leverage they need to protect and elevate the value of enterprise data.

Want to learn more about SAP Business Data Cloud as it applies to your role?  Check out this guide: One Foundation. Every Role. Smarter Insights.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in April
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in April

SAPPHIRE season is upon us 🌴 and the April releases didn't hold back either — plenty of useful features landing across monitoring, task chains, data products, and more.

Learn more in my top features video in April 2026 on YouTube 📺.

In addition, explore the latest updates in our community news blogs and more.
 

 

My top features in April

Update Action for Installed Data Products

Data products installed in an SAP Datasphere space now have an Update action button, available when new minor versions are released. This improvement streamlines the workflow, providing a more efficient and intuitive process for users tasked with keeping data products current. 
➡️  More information Installing Data Products and Data Product Details.

 

BDC Cockpit Data Product Monitoring

As promised, the BDC Cockpit is being continuously improved. Administrators now get significantly better visibility into SAP-managed data product pipelines — this is the first step of several improvements coming over the next months.

The cockpit now shows the full pipeline status at every step, making it easy to identify exactly where issues occur and act before they impact your business.

 

Local Table Partitioning

In the table editor, you can create partitions on existing SAP-Managed local tables stored in a standard space (with SAP HANA Database (Disk and In-Memory) storage) that already contain data. So previously unpartitioned tables can now be partitioned even if they already carry data. This is particularly useful for large-volume tables where partitioning was not possible at initial creation. 
➡️  More information Partitioning Local Tables and Partitioning Local Tables for Intelligent Applications.

 

Spark Configuration

The Limits to the amount of compute resources that Apache Spark applications can consume when running tasks are set with configurations. In addition to using the provided configurations, which are applied by default to new file spaces, you can now change the default configuration for a task activity in each file space, and create your own configurations.

Be aware, that newly provided default configurations for task activities may allocate fewer resources than those used in previous versions (see SAP Note 3726059), tasks might run more slowly even though they consume less compute. If these new default values don't meet your needs, you can change the default configuration for a task activity in each file space. 
➡️  More information.

 

Cloud Connector Support for Google BigQuery

You can now configure private connectivity for Google BigQuery connections through the Cloud Connector. The Cloud Connector routes data traffic through secure tunnels rather than the public internet, allowing you to use replication flows to write data to Google BigQuery target systems that don't have public endpoints. 
➡️  More information.

 

Task Chains Deletion Types

Task chains now support all deletion types for both local tables and Local Tables (file).

The newly supported deletion activities are:

  • Delete All Records
  • Delete Filtered Records

➡️  More information.

 

Semantic Onboarding of Calc Views from HANA Cloud

You can import calculation views from SAP HANA Cloud as remote tables while preserving semantic information. This helps you leverage existing HANA Cloud models without recreating them, keeping their business context and metadata for consistent data modeling across platforms.

Be aware that as a prerequisite, only calculation views deployed in SAP HANA Cloud version QRC 1/2026 or higher can be imported in SAP Datasphere database version 2025.50 or higher.

To ensure that your calculation view can be imported, deploy the calculation view in the source system with SAP Business Data Cloud Integration Mode set to Flat View or Construction Kit.

➡️  More information

  • Importing Entities with Semantics from SAP HANA Cloud
  • SAP HANA Connections
 

Analytic Model — Inherited and Local Properties

A small but useful addition to the analytic model. For analytic models that use another analytic model as source, you can switch on Show Inherited Elements in the properties panel, to display the inherited elements. That makes it easy to distinguish between local and inherited properties. 
➡️  More information.

 

Improved Monitoring Tool Navigation

The Monitoring tool now features an enhanced navigation menu for easier access to key monitoring pages:

  • System and Spaces – Monitor system performance, storage, tasks, and out-of-memory issues across all spaces. Track storage consumption and Apache Spark usage.
  • Capacities Monitoring – Track monthly and daily capacity unit consumption against your subscription and download detailed hourly data.
  • Task Logs – Review task execution history and performance.
  • Expensive Statement Logs – Monitor SQL statements exceeding configured thresholds.
  • Elastic Compute Nodes - Monitor elastic compute nodes key performance figures.
  • Data Integration – Monitor, schedule, and run data replication tasks for remote tables and views. Manage flows and task chains.

That consolidates all the monitoring activities into one menu section. 
➡️  More information.

 

 

SAPPHIRE 2026 — Let's Meet in Orlando

SAP SAPPHIRE 2026 is just around the corner! I'll be in Orlando presenting the introductory session on SAP Business Data Cloud — a 20-minute guided tour of what it is, how it's positioned, and where it fits into SAP's data and analytics strategy. I'm running it twice:

📅 Monday, May 11 | 1:30 PM – 1:50 PM EDT

📅 Tuesday, May 12 | 4:00 PM – 4:20 PM EDT

With 30+ sessions on Business Data Cloud at this year's SAPPHIRE, there's a lot to explore. You'll also find us at the SAP Business Data Cloud booth with six demo stations and hands-on sessions. No excuse not to stop by if you're in Orlando.

Can't make it in person? All content will be available online — and of course, I'll keep publishing the monthly newsletter right here. 
🔗  Session link  |  🔗  My SAPPHIRE blog

 

Webinar Series: The Next Era of Business Data

The last session of the great series has just been streamed on YouTube. In case you could not make it live, all sessions are recorded and I added the links to the recordings below.

🔹Jan 29 | Discover SAP Business Data Cloud in 2026 - recording

🔹Feb 05 | The Smart Path to SAP BW Modernization - recording

🔹Feb 19 | Empowering SAP Datasphere Users with SAP Business Data Cloud Innovations - recording

🔹Feb 26 | From Data to Decisions: Analytics with SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP BDC - recording

🔹Mar 12 | From Data Silos to Business Insight: The Power of BDC Connect - recording

🔹Mar 19 | An Architect's Blueprint for SAP Business Data Cloud as the Foundation for Enterprise AI - recording

🔹Mar 26 | Design. Govern. Deliver. Data Products - Data Product Studio - recording

👉  NEW 🔹 Apr 16 | Breaking Down the SAP BDC Pricing Model - recording

entry.title: From Concept to Practice: Inside My SAP Business Data Cloud Session for Data Teams (BDC2853)
entry.content:
Hi everyone—Kara here 👋

SAPPHIRE is just a few days away, which means a lot of great conversations about data, AI, and how the two actually come together in the real world (not just on slides).

A bit about me: I spend my time at SAP working on all things data and AI - specifically how to turn all the data customers already have into something that actually drives decisions, powers AI, and (ideally) makes life a little easier. A big part of that lately has been helping teams think through what a solid data foundation really looks like when AI is no longer “experimental” – it’s expected.

I am excited to share a bit about my session: SAP Business Data Cloud: Deep dive on business capabilities | BDC2853

If you’ve heard about SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) but are still wondering “okay…but what does it *actually* do for me?”—this session is for you.

We’ll walk through:

  • What’s inside BDC (without the buzzword overload)
  • How the different pieces come together across data, analytics, and AI
  • Who it’s really for – because “everyone” is not a useful answer
  • How key capabilities work in action through short demos

My goal is simple: you should walk away knowing how to connect what BDC offers to your day-to-day work and not just understanding the architecture diagram. Also, good news: you don’t have to be in Orlando to join – this session is available virtually too.

If you will be in Orlando and are already thinking about your data + AI strategy, here are a few other sessions worth checking out: 

      • The data foundation for agentic AI: Implementing a business data fabric | BDC2058V
      • The semantic universe: Dole’s data fabric strategy from farm to fork | BDC2742
      • How Baker Hughes uses SAP BDC and SAP Databricks for AI-enabled operations | PAR1039

Your turn 👇

Before SAPPHIRE kicks off, I’m curious: What’s the hardest part of getting your data ready for AI right now? Is it:

  1. Data quality and governance?
  2. Integration across systems?
  3. Rebuilding business context after replication?
  4. Or something else entirely?

Drop it in the comments—I’d love to hear what’s top of mind (and what’s getting in the way).

Looking forward to seeing you—whether in Orlando or online!

entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026 - Join me in our SAP BDC Sessions!
entry.content:
A couple of words about me: 

Hi, I'm Prakash! I've spent the last 30+ years in the enterprise software world, driven by one passion: turning messy, complex data into something teams can actually trust and act on. I founded Paxata to give customers a simpler, self-service way to prepare data at scale, and before that started Velosel focused on master data management. Most recently, I led DocuSign's Emerging Products & Technology group as Global VP and GM, where my team built the data fabric, AI, search, and analytics platform powering their applications. Today, I have the incredible privilege of leading SAP Business Data Cloud and I can tell you, this is the most exciting chapter yet. We are on a quest to make SAP BDC the enterprise data platform of choice for the agentic era, and we are just getting started!!

I am thrilled to share with you my sessions at SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026. I would love to see you there!

Accelerate insights across your organization with SAP Business Data Cloud | BDC2061
This one is close to my heart. We'll dive into how SAP Business Data Cloud brings SAP data and intelligence together: curated metrics, built-in AI models, and planning capabilities that you can actually put to work today. The focus: accelerating real, high-impact business scenarios like improving working capital or combining workforce insights across SAP and non-SAP sources. This is the power of contextual, business-ready data in action.

Getting to know data products in SAP Business Data Cloud | BDC2747
The fastest path to value? Start with data products that already "know" your business context. In this session, we'll cover what SAP data products are, how they fit into your landscape, and why they matter for both business and IT leaders. If you want to see how SAP Business Data Cloud delivers trusted, AI-ready data at scale—this is the session for you!

Related community managed tags you should subscribe to remain informed about this topic: 
SAP Business Data Cloud

Related community topic pages: 
SAP Business Data Cloud

Looking forward to seeing you: 
If you're at Sapphire Orlando, I would love for you to join me and the team. Across these sessions, we'll get deep into how SAP Business Data Cloud helps you bring together SAP and non-SAP data, work with curated business-ready data products, and put AI and planning capabilities to use for real scenarios - from faster insights to better decisions. Deep gratitude to our incredible customers, partners, and SAP colleagues who make this journey possible. See you in Orlando!!!

entry.title: SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026 - AI database for AI agents and apps: Overview of SAP HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community!

My name is Daniel Dukes, and I’m on SAP’s Technical Product Marketing team. My primary focus is SAP HANA Cloud. Day to day, I spend a lot of time evangelizing the practicality of our database platform at events and through content published in our Data Professionals community. I’ve been in the SAP ecosystem for close to 25 years with the last 15 years at SAP. Much of my tenure has involved working with in-memory data technology in one form or another – from the SAP BW Accelerator to SAP HANA Cloud. 

Database @ SAP Sapphire 2026 in Orlando

I am really looking forward to this year in Orlando. Here is a “sneak peek” of my session at 1:00 PM EDT on Tuesday, May 12 - AI database for AI agents and apps: Overview of SAP HANA Cloud | BDC2188.

The current plan is to walk through the AI-related features in SAP HANA Cloud from two angles. The first topic covers how HANA Cloud can enable AI scenarios - e.g., as the data layer for agents and generative AI services. I also plan to review how HANA Cloud uses AI to improve developer and user productivity. Along with an overview of the solution, I also want to discuss the kinds of use cases this database can empower.

Additionally, here are some other highly recommended SAP HANA Cloud sessions: 

  • Share calculation views and data products across SAP HANA Cloud and SAP BDC | BDC2187 @ 2:00 PM EDT on Tuesday, May 12
  • Build smarter AI agents with an agent memory service in SAP HANA Cloud | BDC 7224 @ 3:00 PM EDT on Tuesday, May 12
  • Semos Cloud delivers culture intelligence with AI agents on SAP HANA Cloud | BDC2632 @ 2:30 PM EDT on Wednesday, May 13
  • SAP HANA Cloud and SAP Business Data Cloud | BDC2947 @ Demo Station

Discover, Connect, Learn, and Grow

If you are ready to explore SAP HANA Cloud beyond these experiences, the quickest hands-on starting point is the SAP HANA Cloud basic trial. It’s a straightforward way to try features yourself without needing a full landscape. After that, I’d suggest working through the hands-on tutorials within the Discovery Center. A third great recommendation is to join other Data Professionals in the SAP Community. Feel free to participate in a place built for those who work with data.

Share Your Thoughts and Experiences

Until we meet in person, let’s correspond through the SAP community. Consider posting a reply to the following question. I’d appreciate any concrete examples (even small ones) shared in the comments section. 

Prompt: Where do you see AI adding real value in your data platform work today?

~

Looking forward to meeting you at my session or on the show floor 😊

entry.title: Reducing Structural Friction in SAP FICO to Unlock Real Business Value
entry.content:
FICO is at the center of all SAP financial transactions and most of what a business has to work with to determine their financial status as well as how they are operating effectively and efficiently. So, in a perfect world, it would be very easy to see what is happening within a business from a financial point of view. But, in reality, this does not always happen that way.

Based on what we have seen over time, rarely has SAP FICO not performed as intended. It works, and it works very well and that's why we keep on using it. However, the initial design of the SAP FICO module can be changed by the evolution of the overall system and by numerous decisions made by many different people throughout the years. In this way, the initial structure becomes increasingly complicated, which results in finance teams having greater challenges with the way they are currently able to conduct their daily operational activities.

SAP FICO defines the organization of financial data in a variety of ways. It's not just about keeping transactions but also about having consistency with how those transactions will be organized, controlled and interpreted throughout your organization. When this organization is inconsistent, the ramifications are far reaching and will impact processes, reporting and decision making outside of the system.

In most cases, consistency doesn't happen over night but is gradually built upon over time from various sources. New customizations are made to meet a specific need in the organization, integrations are added as more systems come online and processes are modified to fit the business need. Each of these changes are fine in isolation; however, over time they start to create a level of structural friction that makes things a little more difficult than necessary.

Frictions are easily noticeable in the sense that they start to create sitations nobody really wants to deal with: financial closes take longer to complete, reports need to be manually adjusted prior to being able to rely on them, teams spend more time reconciling the same numbers rather than analyzing them, and when someone needs a concise snapshot of their financials across multiple areas, what should be simple now becomes considerably more complicated.

One project we did with a large energy firm illustrates the same point. We spent time working with their sap systems to organize and combine finance-related data from the many disparate sources of SAP data. Over years of implementation, a large amount of data built up in an uncoordinated manner, and therefore created many inconsistencies across the company.

At first, it appeared that the issue might be due to the data not being available, but that wasn't entirely accurate. Data was in fact present, but in many different formats, extracted by various means, and not at the same level of detail. Many of the departments did not use the same data, nor did they use it consistently.

Related to that, basic analytic activities became very challenging. Every report had to be reformatted and validated, and the time taken to make decisions moved from immediate to much longer due to the effects of the system. Nevertheless, the organization of data within the system created a high level of friction throughout all levels of the company.

In one of our projects, an example of what we found was that operational data coming from the field was fed directly into SAP (the operating system for supply chain management) and automatically caused maintenance and work order processes to be triggered without manual involvement. After establishing the backbone of that integrated process, the end-to-end flow of information could occur without the need for any further validation thereby allowing the process to be completed more quickly and reliably.

It quickly became apparent that this type of friction is very common and that the reason for this kind of friction is generally structural. At its root, we find a human issue since the evolution of systems tends to be a result of different teams making decisions that don’t follow a single, well-thought-out plan.

To ease this friction, you don’t necessarily need to redesign everything from scratchr. In most instances, it would be more beneficial to simplify what already exists. For example, if you align key data structures, eliminate non-essential complexity, and ensure that integrations are based on a consistent logic, they will have a substantially greater positive effect than providing additional tools or layers on top.

SAP FICO is a strong starting point for running finance at present, and the challenge is whether or not it should continue to add on top of it as well as keep the support models for SAP FICO consistent over time.

When the support models are clear, it is easier to trust the data from SAP FICO, and the processes will function smoothly, allowing the teams to make decisions rather than fixing inconsistencies in the system. When they are not, even the simplest tasks will require additional steps, which makes it harder for a user to see value in the system. Our experience shows that growing complexity causes decreased business value, not increased business value.

entry.title: Your Journey to SAP Data Architect Certification Starts Now
entry.content:
The era of the "all-knowing" architect has arrived, and the blueprint for the enterprise of tomorrow is being written in real-time through the transformative power of Data and AI.

As a professional consultant or architect, you’ve spent your career designing complex systems, but the modern landscape now demands a visionary who can seamlessly bridge the gap between robust infrastructure and the autonomous world of Agentic AI. This is your moment to evolve from a traditional strategist into a high-impact leader capable of orchestrating a data-first world.

 

By enrolling in our newly released learning journeys: Becoming an SAP Data Architect and Mastering SAP Data Architecture. You are securing an undeniable career advantage in an AI-driven economy. These courses offer a structured, rigorous path designed specifically for experts ready to master modern paradigms like Data Mesh, Data Fabric, and Zero-ETL integration. You will gain the technical skills required to design scalable, high-performance data ecosystems that facilitate real-time access and advanced analytics across hybrid cloud environments.

 

Completing this journey validates your expertise on a global stage through industry-recognized certification: the SAP Certified Data Architect – SAP Business Data Cloud. You will master the integration of SAP’s open data ecosystem with external giants like Databricks and Google BigQuery, ensuring you can lead complex AI initiatives with absolute confidence. The future belongs to those who have the skills to build it - claim your seat at the forefront of innovation today.

 

Start your journey today!

entry.title: Customer-Managed Data Products in SAP BDC: What Really Happens When Your Source Is a Datasphere VIEW
entry.content:
Imagine you are a data engineer at a mid-size enterprise running SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition. Your company has just subscribed to SAP Business Data Cloud. The pitch was compelling: bring your SAP data into Databricks with zero copy, no ETL pipelines, no data duplication. You have an existing Datasphere implementation where your team already built a clean, business-ready General Ledger Account master data VIEW. It joins the SKA1 chart-of-accounts table and the SKAT text table, filters to English language, and projects the analytical columns your finance team needs. It took weeks to get right. You want to publish that as a Customer-Managed Data Product and share it to your Enterprise Databricks workspace.

You follow the documented steps. You create a Datasphere HDLF (HANA Data Lake Files) space. You try to point the CMDP directly at your VIEW. And then you discover something nobody told you upfront: you cannot directly share a Datasphere HANA relational VIEW as a Delta-shareable data product. The VIEW must first be materialized into a physical HANA local table. That HANA local table must then be exported to an HDLF Delta Lake file via a Transformation Flow. Only from that HDLF Delta Lake file can the CMDP be published and shared to Databricks via Delta Sharing.

Two physical copies. You thought there would be zero.

Your first reaction is frustration. Your second is a question: did SAP misrepresent the zero-copy architecture? The answer is no, but the explanation requires a level of precision that the marketing material does not always provide. This post provides that precision.

Part 1: The Foundations: What Delta Sharing Actually Guarantees

Before diagnosing the architecture, you need to understand exactly what Delta Sharing is and what its zero-copy promise covers, because this is where the misalignment between expectation and reality originates.

Delta Sharing is an open protocol (originally developed by Databricks, now vendor-neutral) for governed, read-access sharing of data stored in Delta Lake format from a provider to a consumer, without the consumer needing to import or duplicate that data. Delta Lake format means Parquet columnar data files plus a transaction log: a folder of JSON files recording every change operation, stored in a cloud object store (Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2, or Google Cloud Storage depending on your hyperscaler).

The zero-copy guarantee that Delta Sharing makes is specifically and exclusively at the consumer boundary. When Databricks reads a Delta-shared table, the sequence at the protocol level works as follows. Databricks authenticates to the SAP BDC Delta Sharing server using mutual TLS (mTLS, meaning both sides exchange certificates to prove identity) and OpenID Connect (OIDC) for token-based authorization. The server issues a time-limited, pre-signed URL pointing directly to the Parquet files in the HDLF object store. Databricks reads those files directly from HDLF over HTTPS. No data enters Databricks storage. The URL expires. The next query gets fresh URLs. No copy is ever created on the Databricks side.

This is real. It works exactly as described. Zero copy at the consumer boundary is a genuine architectural guarantee that SAP BDC delivers today.

What Delta Sharing has never guaranteed (not in SAP BDC, not in Databricks, not on any platform from any vendor) is the elimination of producer-side preparation steps. Delta Sharing can only share data that already exists in Delta Lake format in an object store. If your data lives in a relational table, a view, a CSV, or any other format, it must be converted to Parquet and Delta Log before Delta Sharing can reference it. That conversion is not duplication in any meaningful business sense. It is a format transformation required by the protocol itself. This same requirement exists on AWS, Azure, GCP, Databricks native, Snowflake, and every other platform implementing Delta Sharing. SAP is not the exception here. SAP is the standard.

The producer-side storage pattern (one copy, or two) depends entirely on a separate decision: where your merge and join logic lives. That is what the rest of this post addresses.

Part 2: The Two HDLF Object Stores in SAP BDC

Before explaining the two architecture options, you need a clear mental model of where data physically lives in SAP BDC. There are two distinct HDLF object store instances and confusing them is a common source of misunderstanding.

The Foundation Services HDLF (FOS) is managed entirely by SAP. It stores SAP-Managed Data Products (the out-of-the-box data products for S/4HANA Finance, Procurement, Sales, HR, and so on). When you activate an Intelligent Application in the BDC Cockpit, SAP automatically populates the relevant data products in the FOS layer. Customers have no direct access to this layer. SAP handles extraction, replication, transformation, and publication invisibly.

The Datasphere HDLF Object Store is managed by the customer within their Datasphere tenant. This is where Customer-Managed Data Products live. Customers configure, populate, transform, and govern this space themselves. It is backed by the same hyperscaler object storage technology as the FOS layer. The difference is ownership, access, and responsibility.

Customer-Managed Data Products can only be published from the Datasphere HDLF object store. They cannot be published directly from the HANA relational space. This is the design constraint that determines everything about the architecture options below.

Part 3: The Real-World Use Case: GL Account Master Data from S/4HANA

The worked example throughout this post is General Ledger Account master data from SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition (PCE), one of the most common CMDP scenarios in enterprise analytics implementations.

In S/4HANA, GL Account master data is stored across two key ABAP tables:

  • SKA1: The chart of accounts segment, holding account number, account type, account group, P&L classification, and balance sheet flag. One record per account per chart of accounts.
  • SKAT: The text segment, holding account short text and long text per language key. Multiple records per account.

A business-ready GL Account master data product for Databricks needs to join SKA1 and SKAT on account number and chart of accounts key, filter to English language, and project the meaningful analytical attributes into a flat, single-row-per-account dataset.

This join is the "merge" that drives the architecture decision. Where you perform it determines whether you end up with one physical copy or two on the SAP side.

Part 4: Option A: Upstream Merge (One Physical Copy)

The core principle: Push the join logic upstream into S/4HANA as a CDS view. By the time data leaves S/4HANA and arrives in Datasphere, it is already a single, merged, business-ready dataset. Datasphere's Replication Flow lands this dataset directly into an HDLF File Space as a Delta Lake local table. One physical copy. No HANA relational space involved at any point.

This option applies to both S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition (PCE) and Public Cloud Edition (CE).

On Private Cloud Edition, your ABAP team builds an extraction-enabled, CDC-capable custom CDS view (for example, ZI_GL_ACCOUNT_MASTER) that performs the SKA1-SKAT join directly in S/4HANA. The view carries the annotation @analytics.dataextraction.enabled: true, making it available in the CDS_EXTRACTION container in Datasphere's Replication Flow. Connectivity from Datasphere to S/4HANA on-premises runs via SAP Cloud Connector, a lightweight outbound-only tunnel that requires no inbound firewall rule changes.

On Public Cloud Edition, the same pattern applies. Custom CDS views are created via the Custom CDS View tile in Fiori Launchpad with a YY1_ prefix. No ABAP IDE or developer access is required. Connectivity is a direct HTTPS API call using the ABAP SQL Service communication arrangement, with no Cloud Connector needed.

The data flow for Option A:

  1. S/4HANA ABAP Layer: Custom CDS view ZI_GL_ACCOUNT_MASTER joins SKA1 and SKAT, filters to language EN, selects analytical columns. Extraction and CDC annotations are activated on the view.
  2. Replication Flow (Datasphere): Reads ZI_GL_ACCOUNT_MASTER from the CDS_EXTRACTION container. The target is an HDLF File Space (Storage Type: SAP HANA Data Lake Files). Data lands in the HDLF inbound buffer.
  3. Merge Task: Runs on a schedule to consolidate the inbound buffer into the HDLF local table (file), creating the Parquet files and Delta transaction log. This is the one and only physical copy on the SAP side.
  4. Transformation Flow (optional): If additional silver layer logic is needed (inactive account filtering, currency enrichment, language fallback), a Spark-based Transformation Flow applies it and writes to a Silver HDLF local table. Still effectively one net dataset if Bronze is transient.
  5. CMDP Publication: A Data Provider Profile with Formations visibility is created. A Customer-Managed Data Product is defined pointing to the HDLF local table as its Product Artifact. The ORD (Open Resource Discovery) metadata descriptor is published to the BDC catalog.
  6. Delta Sharing to Enterprise Databricks: BDC Connect (generally available since October 2025) establishes the two-sided trust between BDC and the customer's existing Databricks workspace. mTLS and OIDC handle authentication. The BDC Delta Sharing server issues signed URLs. Databricks reads Parquet files directly from HDLF over HTTPS. Zero copy at the consumer boundary.

When to choose Option A: New CMDP implementations starting from scratch where the ABAP team can build or extend a CDS view in S/4HANA. High-volume, high-frequency datasets where eliminating the HANA in-memory footprint has meaningful cost impact. Any scenario where you want the leanest possible producer-side architecture.

Part 5: Option B: Datasphere View Merge (Two Physical Copies)

The core principle: Build the join logic as a VIEW inside Datasphere's HANA relational space. This VIEW must be materialized into a physical HANA local table before a Transformation Flow can read it and produce the HDLF Delta Lake file. Two physical copies result on the SAP side: one in HANA relational storage, one in HDLF object storage.

This is the required path when merge logic already lives in a Datasphere HANA VIEW, as it does in most mature Datasphere deployments.

The technical reason this two-copy pattern exists is straightforward: a Datasphere HANA VIEW is a virtual object. It is a saved query definition. It contains no data. A Transformation Flow requires a physical source to read from. The VIEW must therefore be materialized into a physical HANA local table first. Additionally, Replication Flows in Datasphere are designed to read from external source systems (S/4HANA, other ABAP sources, cloud databases). They cannot use a Datasphere HANA VIEW or local table as their source. Directly writing a Datasphere VIEW result to HDLF without materialization is not a supported path today.

This behavior is not unique to SAP. In Oracle, SQL Server, Snowflake, and every relational database system on earth, a view is a virtual object. Materializing before exporting is the standard pattern everywhere.

The data flow for Option B:

  1. S/4HANA ABAP Layer: SKA1 and SKAT are individually extraction-enabled CDS views. Two Replication Flows extract each table separately into Datasphere.
  2. Datasphere HANA Relational Space (Copy 1): SKA1 and SKAT land as separate HANA local tables. This is the first physical copy of the data on the SAP side.
  3. Datasphere HANA VIEW (virtual, no additional copy): A graphical or SQL VIEW in Datasphere joins the two local tables on account number and chart of accounts key, filters to language EN, and projects the analytical columns. This VIEW holds no data.
  4. Materialization to HANA Local Table: The VIEW output persisted into a new physical HANA local table via a Data Flow or view persistency, producing the merged, flat, single-row-per-account dataset in HANA. Still part of Copy 1 (the HANA relational layer).
  5. Transformation Flow to HDLF (Copy 2): A Transformation Flow reads the materialized HANA local table and writes the result as a Delta Lake file (Parquet and transaction log) into the Datasphere HDLF File Space. This is the second physical copy on the SAP side.
  6. CMDP Publication: Same as Option A. Data Provider Profile, CMDP definition, ORD descriptor, BDC catalog publication.
  7. Delta Sharing to Enterprise Databricks: Identical to Option A from this point. BDC Connect, mTLS and OIDC, signed URLs, direct Parquet reads from HDLF. Zero copy at the consumer boundary.

When to choose Option B: When merge and join logic already exists as a Datasphere HANA VIEW and rebuilding it upstream is not justified by the business case. Low-to-medium volume reference data (GL Account master, Cost Center master, Profit Center master) where the HANA memory cost of the materialized table is negligible. When the Datasphere VIEW contains complex business logic (multi-table joins, CASE expressions, aggregations) that would require significant effort to replicate in a CDS view or Spark Transformation Flow.

Part 6: Option A vs. Option B (The Decision Table)

Part 7: How Delta Sharing Actually Transits Data to Enterprise Databricks

For completeness, here is the exact connectivity and transit story for Enterprise Databricks (what SAP calls the "brownfield" scenario, meaning the customer's own independently licensed Databricks workspace, not the SAP Databricks bundled inside BDC). This is the part customers ask about most in architecture reviews.

Connecting BDC to Enterprise Databricks via BDC Connect:

BDC Connect became generally available for all three hyperscale's in October 2025. The connection establishment is a one-time, two-sided handshake:

  1. The Databricks workspace admin generates a connection identifier in Databricks (Data Ingestion > SAP Business Data Cloud tile) and shares it with the SAP BDC admin.
  2. The SAP BDC admin uses that identifier to create a Third Party Connection in BDC and generates an invitation link.
  3. The Databricks admin completes the connection using that invitation link. The confirmation message reads: "Connection to SAP Business Data Cloud was successful."
  4. BDC is now registered as an authorized Delta Sharing provider in the Databricks Unity Catalog. The connection is bidirectional.

The read path (every time Databricks queries a shared table):

  1. Databricks authenticates to the SAP BDC Delta Sharing server using mTLS (mutual Transport Layer Security, where both sides present certificates) and OIDC (OpenID Connect for token-based identity verification).
  2. The Delta Sharing server validates entitlements against the governance policy, covering which data product, which recipient, and which columns or partitions are authorized.
  3. The server issues time-limited pre-signed URLs pointing directly to the specific Parquet files in the HDLF object store that constitute the requested table or delta increment.
  4. Databricks reads those Parquet files directly from HDLF (S3, ADLS Gen2, or GCS) over HTTPS. Data travels encrypted in transit. No SAP middleware sits in this read path.
  5. The signed URLs expire. No persistent copy of the data is created in Databricks storage.

This is zero copy in operational terms. The Parquet files never leave the HDLF object store. Whether those Parquet files were produced via Option A (one copy on the SAP side) or Option B (two copies on the SAP side) is entirely irrelevant to this read path. The consumer experience is identical either way.

Part 8: The Reverse Flow: What Happens When Databricks Sends Enriched Data Back to BDC

A question that comes up in almost every customer architecture discussion: when Databricks runs ML models or analytics on top of the CMDP and sends results back to BDC, does it update the original CMDP? Or is it a new data product?

The answer is unambiguous. It is always a brand new, separate Derived Data Product. The original CMDP is never modified, overwritten, or versioned by the reverse flow.

The data scientist publishes the enriched dataset (say, a GL Account clustering result produced by an ML model) back to BDC using the BDC Connect SDK in a Databricks notebook. Three SDK calls are made in sequence: bdc_connect_client.create_or_update_share() registers the share with its ORD metadata, bdc_connect_client.create_or_update_share_csn() defines the data schema using the CSN (Core Schema Notation) format, and bdc_connect_client.publish_data_product() makes the data product discoverable in the BDC catalog. The new Derived Data Product appears in the BDC Cockpit Catalog and Marketplace as a distinct, independently governed entry, discoverable by SAP Analytics Cloud reports, Datasphere models, and other data products.

The original GL Account master CMDP remains exactly as it was: unchanged, unaware, still serving as a source for other consumers. The Derived Data Product and the original CMDP coexist in the BDC catalog as independent assets with their own lineage, their own governance policies, and their own update schedules. This composability is the foundation of SAP's data product strategy and the direction that Data Product Studio (currently in preview, maturing through 2026) is designed to operationalize at low-code scale.

Closing: The Definitive Framing

The most precise way to describe the SAP BDC zero-copy architecture for Customer-Managed Data Products is this:

SAP BDC delivers genuine zero-copy data sharing to Enterprise Databricks at the consumer boundary via the Delta Sharing protocol. The producer-side pipeline (one physical copy in Option A, two in Option B) prepares the data in Delta Lake format as required by the Delta Sharing protocol. That preparation is not duplication. It is format conversion. It is architecturally equivalent to what every other vendor implementing Delta Sharing requires on every platform, without exception.

The choice between Option A and Option B is not a choice between correct and incorrect architecture. It is a design decision driven by where your merge logic lives and what trade-offs make sense for your specific data product, your S/4HANA deployment type, and your existing Datasphere investment. Both options are fully supported by SAP today. Both deliver genuine zero copy to Databricks. The difference is purely in the producer-side storage footprint and the location of your business logic.

If you are an architect, a CSM, or a customer who has been confused by this, I hope this post has made the picture clear. If your team is navigating the CMDP journey on SAP BDC right now, whether on Private Cloud or Public Cloud, whether starting fresh or working with an existing Datasphere implementation, feel free to reach out in the comments or directly.

Data and Analytics SAP Business Data Cloud RISE with SAP SAP S/4HANA SAP Datasphere SAP S/4HANA SAP Databricks ABAP Development SAP BTP ABAP environment

entry.title: New SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey & SAP Certified Data Architect Certification is now Live
entry.content:
TLDR: The Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Learning Journey is now live on learning.sap and alongside it the New Role Play based SAP Certified Data Architect certification is now available in this learning Journey: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/becoming-a-certified-sap-data-architect

Data Architecture Learning Begins here!

Early in my career, I sat in a room full of architects debating a major platform decision. The conversation covered integration patterns, application landscapes, and total cost of ownership. No one discussed the data. Not the quality of it, not how it would flow, not how it would be governed. We treated data as the responsibility of someone else (master data team and BW team) in a different meeting. Years later, we are seeing AI initiatives slow down because of untrustworthy data, I understand exactly what was missing from that room.

Data architecture has spent years as a supporting discipline important, but rarely the headline. That has changed. The shift to agentic AI has moved data architecture from a back-office concern to a front-line competitive capability.

Agentic AI systems do not simply retrieve information. They reason, plan, and act. They require data that is real-time, contextually grounded, semantically rich, and governed with precision. An agent operating on stale, fragmented, or poorly modelled data does not just produce mediocre outputs it can produce incorrect actions. At enterprise scale, that is a governance and business risk problem, not just a technical one.

The architects who will lead in this environment are those who can hold both conversations at once: the strategic (how data drives business value) and the technical (how to design the platforms, integration patterns, and governance structures that make that value real).  

That is exactly what we have tried to address with the New Data Architecture learning Course (Advanced) and a New Data Architect Certification from SAP

Introducing the Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Course

The Mastering SAP Data Architecture Advanced Learning Journey (course BDCDA2) is now live on learning.sap.com. Some of you might have done ‘Becoming a Data Architect – Foundational Course’ which was launched last year and this new course takes a deeper look at the topics related to Data Architecture.

Across four-unit journey designed for architects who are ready to move beyond foundational concepts and into the architecture decisions, framework synthesis, and platform-specific depth that define effective data leadership in the agentic AI era.

  1.  Unit 1: Architecting Data Strategy for the Agentic AI Era

The journey opens with strategy, because that is where most data initiatives fail first. Unit 1 builds the five foundational pillars of data strategy: Governance, Data literacy, Stewardship, Architecture, and Technology enablement and connects each directly to AI readiness. It examines what agentic AI demands from data pipelines that traditional analytics architectures were not built to provide: real-time streaming, semantic enrichment, and contextual grounding. It then moves to leadership and culture, the organizational factors that determine whether a data strategy survives contact with reality and closes with a framework for measuring data strategy success and analyzing real-world value patterns.

  1. Unit 2: Integrating Frameworks for Data Architecture

Architecture without a framework produces inconsistency. Unit 2 examines the three frameworks that define the professional standard in this field: TOGAF, DAMA-DMBOK, and the SAP Enterprise Architecture Framework and, guides how to synthesize them rather than apply them in isolation. It then applies that synthesis to two of the most significant architectural paradigms in the market today: Data Mesh and Data Fabric.

  1.  Unit 3: Harmonizing SAP Business Data Cloud Within the Open Data Ecosystem

Unit 3 is where strategy and frameworks meet the SAP platform. It begins with BDC Connect and the Delta Sharing protocol the mechanism that enables zero-copy data sharing across ecosystem boundaries and then works through each major integration scenario in the open data ecosystem: Databricks, Snowflake (including all three connectivity options), Microsoft Fabric, and Google Big Query. The unit closes with a dedicated lesson on data federation performance optimization the practical trade-offs between federation, replication, and zero-copy sharing that every architect advising on SAP Business Data Cloud deployments needs to understand cold.

  1. Unit 4: Designing Advanced Data Models and Architecture for SAP Business Data Cloud

The final unit addresses SAP Business Data Cloud as a Business Data Fabric how it implements federation, replication, and zero-copy sharing to connect heterogeneous data sources while preserving business context and semantic coherence. It covers Data Mesh in SAP BDC, including how Datasphere Spaces implement the four Data Mesh principles in practice. It then moves to one of the most consequential topics in enterprise AI architecture: semantic models and knowledge graphs as the foundation for trustworthy AI and RAG-based systems.

 Introducing the SAP Certified Data Architect Certification

Alongside the learning journey, we have launched the SAP Certified Data Architect certification.

The SAP Certified Data Architect is an AI role-play-based certification that assesses how well candidates understand modern data architecture in the context of strategic business success. It covers the five major architectural paradigms: Data Warehouse, Data Lake, Data Mesh, Data Fabric, and Data Lakehouse, alongside the core elements of data maturity: governance frameworks, data security, and business-aligned data strategy. It uses interactive question-and-answer format, assessing the understanding of SAP Data Architecture in the agentic AI era specifically the ability to synthesize and articulate how SAP Business Data Cloud enables governed, semantically coherent data access across hybrid cloud environments.

Note: It is recommended that the candidate completes the 1) Becoming an SAP Data Architect and 2) Mastering SAP Data Architecture courses before attempting the certificate to ensure successful completion.

 Who This Is For

The learning journey and certification are designed for practitioners who are already working in the data and architecture space and want to develop the rigor, depth, and formal recognition to operate at senior level. That includes data architects designing enterprise data platforms, enterprise architects expanding their data architecture competency, analytics and BI leaders responsible for governed data access, and technical decision-makers advising customers on SAP Business Data Cloud strategy.

 If you are starting your data architecture journey, the foundational 'Becoming a Data Architect' learning journey on learning.sap.com is the right starting point. The advanced journey picks up where that one ends.

Getting Started

  1. The Becoming a Certified SAP Data Architect Learning Journey live now for both internal and external learners: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/becoming-a-certified-sap-data-architect
  2. SAP Certified Data Architect certification registration: https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-data-architect-sap-business-data-cloud

A Final Thought

In the agentic AI era, the constraint on enterprise intelligence is rarely the algorithm. It is the architecture underneath it the decisions made about how data is structured, governed, connected, and made meaningful. Those decisions require a specific set of skills. This learning journey and certification exist to build them.

The practitioners who invest in that depth now will be the ones designing the data foundations that AI-enabled enterprises run on. That work is not infrastructure. It is strategy.

Happy Learning!

P.S: A Big Thank you to @JaSoN_Luo , Sabrina Brück and @Basabdatta_Sengupta  for making this course and certification Happen!

SAP Business Data Cloud

entry.title: New SAP Data Architecture Learning Journey & SAP Certified Data Architect Certification is now Live
